metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for closing the async `httpx.AsyncClient` after `run_sync()` returns in the sync `PydanticAIRunner.run()` context — e.g. wrap the client construction+use+teardown inside `asyncio.run()` alongside the agent call, or use `httpx.Client` (sync) for the transport and wrap it, or pass `http_client` into `AsyncAnthropic` and rely on pydantic-ai/anthropic SDK's own lifecycle management.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the mechanism for closing the `httpx.AsyncClient` (an async resource) from the synchronous caller after `run_sync()` has already created and destroyed its own asyncio event loop, leaving the lifecycle contract under-specified and the 'no unclosed-client warning' AC potentially unachievable with a naive try/finally.
findings.location: ## What / AsyncClient lifecycle section; AC 'AsyncClient does not leak (no unclosed-client warning across a run)'
findings.suggested_fix: Pass the `httpx.AsyncClient` into `AsyncAnthropic(http_client=...)` and do NOT independently aclose it — the Anthropic SDK (and pydantic-ai via `AnthropicProvider`) owns the lifecycle of a client it received and will close it when the provider is garbage-collected, which is bounded to the run since the model is constructed per-run. Add a comment explaining this ownership transfer. Alternatively, wrap the entire per-run block (client construction + agent.run_sync() + client close) in a single `asyncio.run()` call, but this requires restructuring the sync entry point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact mechanism for closing the `httpx.AsyncClient` from within the synchronous `PydanticAIRunner.run()` method (e.g., use `httpx.Client` sync wrapper with a sync transport, or use `anyio.from_thread.run_sync` / `asyncio.run()` guarded by loop-running detection, or document that `run_sync` executes in a new event loop whose teardown closes the client).
findings.finding: The plan creates `httpx.AsyncClient` (which is async-native) inside the synchronous `PydanticAIRunner.run()` method and calls `aclose()` for teardown, but `aclose()` is a coroutine — calling it from the synchronous `run()` context requires an explicit event-loop bridge (e.g., `asyncio.run(client.aclose())` or `loop.run_until_complete(client.aclose())`) that the plan does not specify. Without this, the `try/finally` teardown either silently drops the coroutine or raises a RuntimeError, defeating the 'no unclosed-client warning' AC.
findings.location: Plan section 'AsyncClient lifecycle'; AC line 'the AsyncClient does not leak (no unclosed-client warning across a run)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for closing the `httpx.AsyncClient` from within the synchronous `PydanticAIRunner.run()` — e.g., use `httpx.Client` (sync) with `AsyncTenacityTransport` wrapped in a sync adapter, or use `client.close()` if available on AsyncClient, or document that pydantic-ai's `run_sync` runs the event loop so `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(client.aclose())` is usable post-`run_sync`.
findings.finding: The `httpx.AsyncClient` lifecycle teardown mechanism is unspecified for a synchronous call context. The plan states the client is 'explicitly closed at the end of the run (try/finally or an `aclose()` on teardown)' — but `runner.py:run()` is a synchronous method (`run_sync` internally manages an event loop). `AsyncClient.aclose()` is a coroutine and cannot be called from synchronous code without an explicit event loop reference. The plan does not specify HOW the async client is closed from within the sync runner (e.g., `asyncio.run(client.aclose())`, a sync `__exit__` shim, a context manager, or relying on the GC finalizer).
findings.location: What → AsyncClient lifecycle (concrete) section; Scope bullets

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None` default with a concrete sensible fallback (e.g. `httpx.Timeout(timeout=60.0)` matching `llm_retry_max_wait_s`) so hung connections are bounded even before hoopoe lands, and the TimeoutException retry arm is always active.
findings.finding: When story chief-contained-hoopoe has not yet landed, http_timeout defaults to None, meaning httpx.AsyncClient is constructed with no per-attempt timeout. A stalled TCP connection (server accepts but never responds) will block the run indefinitely — the retry mechanism cannot self-heal because httpx.TimeoutException never fires without a configured timeout.
findings.location: ### Timeout semantics + the hoopoe merge interface / `_build_retrying_anthropic_model` signature / `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = None`
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level `_DEFAULT_HTTP_TIMEOUT = httpx.Timeout(connect=10.0, read=120.0, write=10.0, pool=5.0)` and use it as the default: `http_timeout: httpx.Timeout | None = _DEFAULT_HTTP_TIMEOUT`. Document that hoopoe overrides this default rather than supplying the only timeout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/env-vars.md` to the Files list and add a sentence in the Config keys section (or a dedicated 'Docs' bullet) describing the env-vars.md regeneration step and the `test_config_llm.py` `_clean` fixture update as explicit deliverables.
findings.finding: The AC requires `docs/env-vars.md` to be regenerated for the two new env vars and `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture to list them, but neither the plan body nor the Files section mentions `docs/env-vars.md` as a deliverable or describes the _clean fixture update as work to be done.
findings.location: AC: 'docs/env-vars.md is regenerated for REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS/REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S (the CI drift gate passes); test_config_llm.py\'s _clean fixture lists them.' vs Files section and plan body.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Config keys section, append: 'Also regenerate `docs/env-vars.md` (the CI drift gate verifies this) and add `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` / `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S` to the `_clean` fixture in `test_config_llm.py`.' Add `docs/env-vars.md` to the Files list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a two-line revert instruction: remove the `[retries]` extra and restore the pre-refactor provider construction) or describe an expand-contract deployment sequence so the behavioral change is reversible under pressure.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges and justifies replacing SDK-managed retries with transport-layer retries (an explicit behavioral change) but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — if the new transport approach introduces a regression (e.g., a tenacity/httpx interaction bug), there is no described mechanism to revert to a working state.
findings.location: What section: 'Opt-out semantics (precise)' and 'The single unified Anthropic construction'; Scope section.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note: 'To revert: (1) restore `pydantic-ai-slim[anthropic]>=1,<2` in pyproject.toml, (2) restore the pre-refactor AnthropicProvider construction in runner.py (SDK-managed provider for the normal path, custom base_url for loopback). The config keys are additive and need not be removed. This restores SDK default retry behavior (2 retries) at the cost of losing transport-level retry safety.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete observability hook in the plan (e.g. 'use a before_sleep callback on RetryConfig that logs attempt number, status/exception, and wait duration at WARNING') so the implementation and the caplog assertion target the same code path.
findings.finding: The retry observability implementation is described with an unresolved 'OR': 'via the transport's validate_response/wait hooks OR a before_sleep callback on the RetryConfig.' An executing agent must choose one mechanism to implement; without a stated default, two developers working on related tests could assume different hooks, causing test brittleness or duplicate logging.
findings.location: 'Retry observability' section — 'via the transport's validate_response/wait hooks or a before_sleep callback on the RetryConfig'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the OR with: 'Register a before_sleep=before_sleep_log(logger, logging.WARNING) (tenacity built-in) plus a custom before_sleep that extracts status code and Retry-After from the exception; the validate_response callback raises but does not log (logging is the before_sleep's responsibility).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the ADR stub path claim: 0038 already exists (`docs/adr/0038-governance-artifacts.md`), so 0037 is a gap (not 'next free'); confirm the intended stub filename with the ADR sequence owner (blackbear) before the AC test hard-codes the path.
findings.finding: The plan states 'docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md (0037 is the next free number)' as the ADR stub path, but ADR 0038 already exists in the repo (docs/adr/0038-governance-artifacts.md, dated 2026-07-09), meaning 0037 is a gap in the sequence rather than the true 'next' number. While 0037 has no file and is technically still free, any contributor following the plan's numbering logic after this story would collide at 0039+, not 0038.
findings.location: ADR section: 'docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md (0037 is the next free number — 0026 is already taken by two existing ADRs; blackbear, the sole ADR/docs owner, finalizes/renumbers if needed)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ADR section to read: 'writes a short ADR stub at `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md` (0037 is currently unoccupied — 0038 already exists; blackbear finalizes/renumbers if needed)' — or just drop the 'next free number' claim since the plan already delegates the final number to blackbear.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture update is a deliverable (not pre-existing): the fixture currently does NOT include `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` or `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`; add both to the fixture's env-name list as part of this story.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'every existing LLM OPERATIONAL knob (`timeout_s`, `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`) already lives on `LLMConfig` with a `REBAR_LLM_*` env fallback' as justification for placing the two new retry keys there. This is confirmed. However, the plan also says '`test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture lists them' (as part of the AC), but the actual `_clean` fixture in `test_config_llm.py` lists only the currently existing env vars (lines 29-43) and does NOT include `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` or `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S`. The plan treats the fixture update as an AC deliverable, which is correct — but if a reader mistakes the AC wording as 'the fixture already lists them', that is a false premise.
findings.location: AC: '`docs/config.md` documents both keys; `docs/env-vars.md` is regenerated for `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS`/`REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S` (the CI drift gate passes); `test_config_llm.py`'s `_clean` fixture lists them.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC bullet, rewrite as: 'update `test_config_llm.py`\'s `_clean` fixture to add `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_ATTEMPTS` and `REBAR_LLM_RETRY_MAX_WAIT_S` to the monkeypatched env list (currently absent from the fixture at lines 29-43).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC (or an explicitly documented rationale for its absence) that exercises the new unified retrying-client construction end-to-end against the live Anthropic API (e.g. a canary/smoke test gated on a real API key in CI), or explicitly document why the offline spike is sufficient (e.g. SDK version pinned, construction-time assertion fully covers the wiring) so a reviewer can evaluate the trade-off.
findings.finding: The unified retrying-client cutover is a new default production code path (all real Anthropic model calls), but every AC that exercises this path uses offline/injected-mock HTTP responses (the spike and integration tests intercept httpx before it reaches Anthropic). No AC exercises the new construction end-to-end against the live Anthropic API with a real key, so it is possible for all offline tests to pass while the production wiring silently misfires (e.g. AnthropicProvider rejects the injected client, or max_retries=0 is ignored in a future SDK version).
findings.location: AC #2 (spike integration test) and AC #3 (construction-time guard) — both explicitly offline/mocked
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'When ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is available in CI, a smoke-test calls the live Anthropic endpoint through the unified helper and asserts max_retries==0 and the transport is AsyncTenacityTransport on the returned client (construction-time guard); if no key is available the test is skipped with a documented rationale.' This exercises the real SDK wiring path without requiring an injected 429.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'if a shared/cached client is used' hedge and state only the decided lifecycle: per-run construction inside PydanticAIRunner.run() with explicit aclose() in a try/finally, matching the 'Notes on prior art' bullet which correctly states the per-run decision.
findings.finding: The AsyncClient lifecycle section contains a contradictory hedge: it first states 'Construct it per-run ... bounded to the run's lifetime' (a clear decision) and then adds 'if a shared/cached client is used, its shutdown is handled' — introducing an alternative design the executor might choose, making the lifecycle ownership decision ambiguous.
findings.location: 'AsyncClient lifecycle' section (both the prose section and the 'Notes on prior art' bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Delete 'if a shared/cached client is used, its shutdown is handled' from the prose section; keep only 'The httpx.AsyncClient is created together with the AnthropicModel inside PydanticAIRunner.run() and is bounded to that run; it is explicitly closed at the end of the run (try/finally or an aclose() on teardown).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete proving command for the tenacity installation AC, e.g. 'python -c "import tenacity; assert tenacity.__version__ >= \'9\'" runs in CI after make install' or a pytest marker that imports AsyncTenacityTransport and fails with ImportError if the extra is absent.
findings.finding: The AC 'make install provisions tenacity (asserted)' does not specify the assertion mechanism — it is unclear whether 'asserted' means a CI step, a pytest fixture, a shell command, or a manual verification, leaving the proving command undefined.
findings.location: AC #1: 'make install provisions tenacity (asserted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC #1: 'Verified by: python -c "from pydantic_ai.retries import AsyncTenacityTransport; import tenacity" exits 0 in CI after make install; or equivalently, a test_imports.py fixture that imports the symbol and is collected by make test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge test_executor_tripwire.py (which bans `tenacity` in executor/interpreter scope) in the Scope or Files section, and confirm via an AC or implementation note that all tenacity/AsyncTenacityTransport imports in runner.py remain lazy (inside the method body) and do not propagate to executor.py or interpreter.py.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate test_executor_tripwire.py as an affected consumer. That test bans 'tenacity' from executor.py and interpreter.py via an AST import scan. While the plan correctly places tenacity use inside runner.py (not executor/interpreter), if any import of the retrying transport leaks into executor.py or interpreter.py (e.g. via a shared helper import chain), the tripwire fires and the build breaks with no clear attribution to this story's change.
findings.location: tests/unit/workflow/test_executor_tripwire.py:20-30 / Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Scope section: 'Note: tests/unit/workflow/test_executor_tripwire.py bans tenacity from executor.py/interpreter.py; all pydantic_ai.retries imports in runner.py must remain inside method bodies (already lazy per the optionality contract) — verify make test does not trip this tripwire.' No code change is needed if the lazy-import discipline is maintained.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the ADR rationale: replace '0026 is already taken by two existing ADRs' with '0036 is already taken by two existing ADRs; 0038 is also occupied, making 0037 the next free slot'.
findings.finding: The plan states '0026 is already taken by two existing ADRs' as the rationale for choosing 0037 as the next free slot. The actually doubly-occupied slot is 0036 (0036-acli-429-rate-limit-backoff.md and 0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md). While 0037 is genuinely free and the destination path is correct, the incorrect slot reference (0026 vs 0036) in the rationale could confuse an implementor checking the numbering or a reviewer auditing the ADR sequence.
findings.location: ADR section / 'docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md (0037 is the next free number — 0026 is already taken by two existing ADRs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ADR section text to: '…docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md (0037 is the next free number — 0036 is already doubly-occupied by 0036-acli-429-rate-limit-backoff.md and 0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md, and 0038-governance-artifacts.md is also taken; blackbear, the sole ADR/docs owner, finalizes/renumbers if needed)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit the spike (or a minimal reproducible version of it) to the repo (e.g., `docs/experiments/` or `tests/integration/spike_transport_retry.py`) so the 'GO' result is persisted and reproducible independently of the implementer's local environment.
findings.finding: The spike file referenced as 'the load-bearing proof' (tmp/spike.py) is not committed to the repository and cannot be reproduced by a reviewer or CI. The plan calls it 'the load-bearing proof' and 're-run offline', but searching the repo yields zero hits for the file path or its content.
findings.location: ## Verified library behavior section — 'SPIKE (the load-bearing proof; tmp/spike.py, re-run offline)'
findings.suggested_fix: Move `tmp/spike.py` to `docs/experiments/transport-retry-spike/spike.py` (or directly to the integration test file the AC calls for) and add it to version control before or alongside this story's implementation, so the proof is repo-permanent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit the spike script (e.g., `tests/spikes/test_transport_retry_spike.py` or `docs/experiments/`) so the GO result is reproducible and inspectable, and pin or document the exact pydantic-ai version at which the symbols were verified.
findings.finding: The plan claims `pydantic_ai.retries.AsyncTenacityTransport`, `wait_retry_after`, and `RetryConfig` exist as importable symbols under the `[retries]` extra of `pydantic-ai-slim`, but these are entirely new library surfaces with zero existing usage in the rebar codebase. The spike file `tmp/spike.py` referenced in the plan is absent from the repo snapshot and cannot be inspected or re-run by a reviewer. The experiment log output is inline prose, not a committed artifact.
findings.location: Plan section 'Verified library behavior (experiment log)'; spike reference 'tmp/spike.py, re-run offline'
findings.suggested_fix: Move `tmp/spike.py` to `tests/spikes/test_transport_retry_spike.py` (skipped unless an env var is set) and commit it; add a comment with the pydantic-ai version verified against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the runner.py exception-handler section (co-located with the retry construction) whether this story's runner.py `except Exception` block must explicitly unwrap `tenacity.RetryError` before forming `LLMUnavailableError`, or confirm that `reraise=True` guarantees the original exception always reaches runner.py directly — do not defer this decision solely to story mamba.
findings.finding: The plan does not co-locate a decision rule for whether this story's runner.py `except Exception` handler (line 353) must handle `tenacity.RetryError` before wrapping into `LLMUnavailableError`. The plan states `reraise=True` re-raises the original exception on exhaustion, then adds 'story mamba's classifier additionally unwraps a tenacity.RetryError defensively for the edge paths — complementary.' This is a cross-story protocol gap: the runner.py exception handler this story modifies is never told whether it can see a bare `tenacity.RetryError` (e.g., if `reraise=True` is not honored in some path), and if so, what to do with it. The 'mamba handles it defensively' framing defers the decision to a different story, leaving this story's runner.py handler under-specified.
findings.location: What → Retry config (proven by the spike) / `reraise=True` clause; also runner.py:353 `except Exception` handler
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Construction-time guard' or 'Retry config' section: 'Because `reraise=True` is set, `tenacity.RetryError` will NOT reach runner.py's `except Exception` handler on exhaustion — the original `httpx.HTTPStatusError` / `httpx.TimeoutException` / `httpx.NetworkError` propagates directly. runner.py needs no RetryError unwrap; mamba's defensive unwrap covers only edge paths in other callers.' This makes the protocol co-located and unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the ADR stub path in the plan (and the AC) to reflect the actual next-free ADR number (0039, since 0038 already exists), or soften the AC to assert the stub exists at 'the next available ADR number' rather than hard-coding `0037`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies the ADR stub path as `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md`, asserting '0037 is the next free number — 0026 is already taken by two existing ADRs.' However, the actual ADR directory contains `0038-governance-artifacts.md`, meaning 0038 is already in use and 0037 is either already taken or a gap that would leave the numbering inconsistent. The AC makes the stub path a concrete acceptance criterion, so the wrong path would cause the AC check to pass on a mis-numbered file.
findings.location: What → ADR section; AC bullet 'an ADR stub exists at the placeholder path'; Files list: `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md`
findings.suggested_fix: Change `docs/adr/0037-transport-retry.md` → `docs/adr/0039-transport-retry.md` throughout the plan (What → ADR section, Files list, AC bullet), since `0038-governance-artifacts.md` already occupies the 0038 slot.

findings.finding: AC #2 requires that a 500-with-no-Retry-After → 200 sequence is retried via `fallback_strategy=None` default backoff, but the spike only tested the 429+Retry-After path. The plan's prose claim that `fallback_strategy=None` means 'use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present' is not covered by any experiment-log entry. In pydantic-ai's `wait_retry_after`, `fallback_strategy=None` can mean 'do not retry when header is absent' rather than 'fall back to a default wait strategy,' which would cause the 500-no-header path to propagate immediately and fail AC #2.
findings.location: AC #2 / 'Retry config (proven by the spike)' / Experiment log
findings.suggested_fix: In the spike, add a second test sequence: mock a transport that returns HTTP 500 (no Retry-After header) then HTTP 200, run `agent.run_sync()` with the same transport, assert `requests == 2`. Log the result as experiment entry [7]. If this fails, replace `fallback_strategy=None` with `fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=llm_retry_max_wait_s)` in the RetryConfig spec.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` means 'use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present,' but this specific semantic is unverified: the experiment log [2] only confirms the parameter *name* exists, and the spike result only covers the `429+Retry-After→200` path (both paths show `requests=2 output='SPIKE_OK'`). No spike output or log entry verifies the `500/no-Retry-After→200` path. In pydantic-ai's implementation `fallback_strategy=None` plausibly means 'no fallback strategy' (zero wait or immediate raise when header is absent), not 'apply tenacity default exponential backoff' — which would cause 5xx retries to fire with no inter-attempt delay or to not fire at all, both of which defeat the self-healing goal.
findings.location: What > Retry config (proven by the spike) — `fallback_strategy=None` description; AC bullet 2: '500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In the spike, add a third mock-server sequence that returns HTTP 500 with no `Retry-After` header on the first attempt and 200 on the second, time the inter-attempt gap, and log it. If the gap is ~0s, change the RetryConfig to `wait=wait_retry_after(fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=<llm_retry_max_wait_s>), max_wait=<llm_retry_max_wait_s>)` and update the plan/AC accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `wait_retry_after(fallback_strategy=None)` activates 'tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present,' making 500/502/503/504 (which never send Retry-After) self-healing with a backoff. The spike only exercised the 429+Retry-After path (where the header IS present and the wait behavior is header-driven), leaving the no-header path untested. In pydantic-ai's implementation, `fallback_strategy=None` most likely means NO fallback strategy — i.e., zero wait (or immediate propagation) when no header is present — not a default exponential backoff. Experiment log [2] confirms the parameter name exists but does not verify the None-semantics. If the plan's interpretation is wrong, all 5xx retries (500, 502, 503, 504) will fire with zero inter-attempt delay, producing a retry storm against an overloaded provider rather than a self-heal, AND AC #2's 500/no-header assertion may be unachievable as written.
findings.location: Retry config section: 'fallback_strategy DEFAULTS to None … None means "use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present"'; AC #2: 'a 500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff'; Experiment log [2]
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `fallback_strategy=None` with `fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=<llm_retry_max_wait_s>)` (a tenacity built-in) to guarantee non-zero backoff for 5xx responses that carry no `Retry-After` header. Update experiment log [2] with a concrete spike result showing inter-attempt delay > 0 on the no-header path. If the spike confirms None DOES provide backoff, document the exact mechanism (e.g., which tenacity default is applied and its parameters) so the AC can assert wait duration, not just retry occurrence.

findings.finding: The plan describes `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` as meaning "use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present," but in tenacity's actual `wait_retry_after` implementation, `fallback_strategy=None` means there is NO fallback strategy — the wait is effectively 0 (immediate retry), not tenacity's default exponential backoff. The AC for the 500-no-header path says it is "retried via the `fallback_strategy=None` default backoff" but does not assert any minimum wait time, so the test cannot distinguish a correct backoff from an immediate (potentially thundering-herd) retry. This sends the implementer in two directions: the body implies exponential backoff will occur for 500s without a Retry-After header, while the library will silently do immediate retries.
findings.location: Retry config section body + AC line: '500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `fallback_strategy=None` with `fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=llm_retry_max_wait_s)` so 500s without a Retry-After header get genuine backoff. Update the AC to assert wait duration > 0 for the no-header path.

findings.finding: The plan characterises `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` as 'use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present', but in pydantic-ai's implementation None means NO fallback strategy — i.e., zero-wait (or immediate raise) when the response carries no Retry-After header. If correct, retries on 500/502/503/504 (which never send Retry-After) would fire with zero inter-attempt wait, hammering an already-overloaded server. The AC asserts the 500->200 sequence IS retried but does not assert a non-zero wait between attempts, so a retry storm would pass the AC undetected.
findings.location: What > Retry config section; AC line: '500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the RetryConfig to `wait=wait_retry_after(fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s), max_wait=cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s)` so that when no Retry-After header is present the transport still backs off exponentially rather than retrying immediately.

findings.finding: `fallback_strategy=None` in `wait_retry_after` does not mean 'use tenacity's default backoff'; in tenacity's actual implementation it means NO fallback strategy — zero inter-attempt wait when no Retry-After header is present. Status codes 500, 502, 503, 504 never send Retry-After, so all four retries on server-error responses would fire back-to-back with no delay, hammering an already-overloaded provider. The spike only asserts retry OCCURS (requests=2), not that inter-attempt wait > 0, so this defect passes the AC undetected.
findings.location: What > Retry config section: 'fallback_strategy=None … None means "use tenacity's default backoff when no Retry-After header is present"'; AC line 2: '500(NO Retry-After)->200 sequence is retried via the fallback_strategy=None default backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the RetryConfig wait to: `wait=wait_retry_after(fallback_strategy=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, max=cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s), max_wait=cfg.llm_retry_max_wait_s)`. This honors Retry-After when present and falls back to exponential backoff when absent, capped at the same operator-configured ceiling. Update AC #2 to assert that at least one inter-attempt sleep occurred during the 500-no-header test.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the `httpx.AsyncClient` timeout (injected at construction time for the per-run client) is a per-attempt timeout or a total-operation timeout across all retry attempts. Since `cfg.timeout_s` is also wired via `model_settings['timeout']` at runner.py:286 (a pydantic-ai/SDK-layer signal), there are two timeout signals in play simultaneously. If the httpx client timeout is per-attempt, `llm_retry_max_attempts=4` multiplies the operator's wall-clock budget by up to 4x; if it is total-across-retries, a slow first attempt plus retry-after wait may exhaust the timeout before recovery occurs. The interaction is unspecified and affects whether `REBAR_LLM_TIMEOUT` still bounds the run.
findings.location: What > AsyncClient lifecycle section; What > The single unified Anthropic construction (httpx.AsyncClient construction); Scope section: 'story chief-contained-hoopoe passes its httpx.Timeout INTO this same httpx.AsyncClient'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
